analogy: Think of "Breathing and Exchange of Gases" through the chapter's own opening images: LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217 Movement is one of the significant features of living beings. Animals and plants exhibit a wide range of movements. Streaming of protoplasm in the unicellular organisms like Amoeba is a simple form of movement. Movement of cilia, flagella and tentacles are... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Breathing and Exchange of Gases is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that lOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217 Movement is one of the significant features of living beings.

The text explains that animals and plants exhibit a wide range of movements.

The text explains that streaming of protoplasm in the unicellular organisms like Amoeba is a simple form of movement.

The text explains that movement of cilia, flagella and tentacles are shown by many organisms.

The text explains that human beings can move limbs, jaws, eyelids, tongue, etc.

The text explains that some of the movements result in a change of place or location.

The text explains that such voluntary movements are called locomotion.

The text explains that walking, running, climbing, flying and swimming are all some forms of locomotory movements.

The text explains that locomotory structures need not be different from those affecting other types of movements.

The text explains that for example, in Paramoecium, cilia helps in the movement of food through cytopharynx and in locomotion as well.

The text explains that hydra can use its tentacles for capturing its prey and also use them for locomotion.

The text explains that we use limbs for changes in body postures and locomotion as well.

The text explains that the above observations suggest that movements and locomotion cannot be studied separately.

The text explains that the two may be linked by stating that all locomotions are movements but all movements are not locomotions.

The text explains that methods of locomotion performed by animals vary with their habitats and the demand of the situation.

The text explains that however, locomotion is generally for search of food, shelter, mate, suitable breeding grounds, favourable climatic conditions or to escape from enemies/predators.

The text explains that they are involuntary in nature as the nervous system does not control their activities directly.

The text explains that the 8th, 9th and 10th pairs of ribs do not articulate directly with the sternum but join the seventh rib with the help of hyaline cartilage.

The text explains that (e) Sternum is present on the ventral side of the body.

The text explains that write the difference between : (a) Actin and Myosin (b) Red and White muscles (c) Pectoral and Pelvic girdle 6.

The text explains that match Column I with Column II : Column I Column II (a) Smooth muscle (i) Myoglobin (b) Tropomyosin (ii) Thin filament (c) Red muscle (iii) Sutures (d) Skull (iv) Involuntary 7.

The text explains that what are the different types of movements exhibited by the cells of human body?

The text explains that how do you distinguish between a skeletal muscle and a cardiac muscle?

The text explains that name the type of joint between the following:- (a) atlas/axis (b) carpal/metacarpal of thumb (c) between phalanges (d) femur/acetabulum (e) between cranial bones (f) between pubic bones in the pelvic girdle 10.

The text explains that fill in the blank spaces: (a) All mammals (except a few) have __________ cervical vertebra.

The text explains that (b) The number of phalanges in each limb of human is __________ (c) Thin filament of myofibril contains 2 ‘F’ actins and two other proteins namely __________ and __________.

The text explains that (d) In a muscle fibre Ca++ is stored in __________ (e) __________ and __________ pairs of ribs are called floating ribs.

The text explains that (f) The human cranium is made of __________ bones.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17: Breathing and Exchange of Gases

revision_bullets: LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217 Movement is one of the significant features of living beings. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Streaming of protoplasm in the unicellular organisms like Amoeba is a simple form of movement. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Movement of cilia, flagella and tentacles are shown by many organisms. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Human beings can move limbs, jaws, eyelids, tongue, etc. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Some of the movements result in a change of place or location. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Walking, running, climbing, flying and swimming are all some forms of locomotory movements. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Locomotory structures need not be different from those affecting other types of movements. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
For example, in Paramoecium, cilia helps in the movement of food through cytopharynx and in locomotion as well. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
Hydra can use its tentacles for capturing its prey and also use them for locomotion. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)
We use limbs for changes in body postures and locomotion as well. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch17 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217 and 17.1 TYPES OF MOVEMENT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Breathing and Exchange of Gases; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Breathing and Exchange of Gases through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217 and 17.1 TYPES OF MOVEMENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

options: 218 BIOLOGY Some specialised cells in our body like macrophages and leucocytes in blood exhibit amoeboid movement.

Cytoskeletal elements like microfilaments are also involved in amoeboid movement.
Ciliary movement occurs in most of our internal tubular organs which are lined by ciliated epithelium.
